description: This chart plots regression coefficients (r_i, unit: GPa) for the effect of alloying elements on the elastic modulus of martensitic and ferritic steels, with phase composition and microstructure neglected. The x-axis arranges elements in decreasing order of significance (r_i/σ_i), including Fe, Cr, Mo, Si, Ni, Mn, Cu, C. Solid symbols with error bars represent calculated regression coefficients, where error bars indicate the standard error of each coefficient; open circles correspond to literature data. The results show that Cr, Mo, and Si increase the elastic modulus of the studied steels, while C and Ni decrease it. Mn and Cu are statistically insignificant at the concentration ranges investigated. The regression model achieves R²=0.96 with a standard error of 1.2 GPa, supporting that the derived coefficients can be used to predict the elastic modulus of steels with known chemical compositions.

This plot shows regression analysis results of compositional influence on the elastic modulus of ferritic steels, with elemental concentrations in ferrite and cementite content pre-calculated via Thermo-Calc. The x-axis presents elements/cementite sorted by decreasing significance of $r_i/\sigma_i$, including Fe, cementite, Cr, Mo, Si, Mn, Cu. The y-axis represents regression coefficient $r_i$ with unit GPa: black horizontal bars with error bars are the obtained regression coefficients with standard errors, while open circles are literature data. It is observed that Mn and Si exhibit the highest positive regression coefficients (~5.5 GPa), followed by Mo (~4 GPa), Cr (~2.5 GPa), Fe and cementite (~2 GPa), Cu (~2.7 GPa with large standard error thus low significance). Cementite shows negligible effect on ferritic steel elastic modulus. The derived regression coefficients can be applied to predict elastic modulus of ferritic steels with known compositions.

This chart presents regression coefficients (r_i, unit: GPa) for the effect of different components (alloying elements and carbides) on the elastic modulus of austenitic steels, with components sorted in decreasing order of significance r_i/σ_i on the x-axis. Each data point includes error bars representing the standard deviation of the corresponding regression coefficient. Identified components include Fe, Cr, Ni, M₂₃C₆, NbC, TiC, N, and Mo. Results show that N, TiC, NbC, and Cr increase the elastic modulus of austenitic steels, while Ni and Mo decrease it; M₂₃C₆ shows no significant effect on elastic modulus despite its statistical significance in composition analysis. Nitrogen has a large regression coefficient but very high deviation, indicating low significance due to limited tested steel batches containing high N content. This figure supports that the obtained regression coefficients can be used to predict the elastic modulus of austenitic steels with known composition, and the corresponding regression model has an R² value of 0.83 and a standard error of 2.9 GPa.
